perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is governed and construed under the laws of the State of Missouri and there are no understandings, agreements or representations, express or implied, not specified herein.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is governed and construed under the laws of the State of Delaware and there are no understandings, agreements or representations, express or implied, not specified herein.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Missouri to Delaware could affect the enforceability of specific clauses, especially if Missouri law offers stronger protections regarding confidential information or contract interpretation in this context.
perturbation.law_explanation: Missouri law might have specific provisions regarding choice of law clauses, particularly consumer contracts or contracts significantly performed in Missouri. Substituting Delaware law without considering these provisions may render the choice of law clause unenforceable under Missouri law if it violates a fundamental policy of Missouri.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Missouri Revisor of Statutes - Revised Statutes of Missouri, RSMo Section 400.1-301 ☰ Revisor of Missouri Constitution Committee Publications Other Links Help / FAQ Appendices and Tables Words Section None Or And Not Near Do search Help This chapter only Title XXVI TRADE AND COMMERCE Chapter 400 < > Effective - 28 Aug 2017 400.1-301. Territorial applicability — parties' power to choose applicable law. — (a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), this chapter applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of this chapter specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) section 400.2-402 ; (2) sections 400.2A-105 and 400.2A-106 ; (3) section 400.4-102 ; (4) section 400.4A-507 ; (5) section 400.5-116 ; (6) section 400.8-110 ; (7) sections 400.9-301 through 400.9-307 . ­­-------- (L. 2017 H.B. 34) ---- end of effective 28 Aug 2017 ---- use this link to bookmark section 400.1-301 Click here for the Reorganization Act of 1974 - or - Concurrent Resolutions Having Force & Effect of Law In accordance with Section 3.090 , the language of statutory sections enacted during a legislative session are updated and available on this website on the effective date of such enacted statutory section. perturbation.justification: The change from Missouri to Delaware law presents a structural flaw because it introduces a legal contradiction by potentially undermining consumer protection. The law snippet (Missouri Revisor of Statutes, RSMo Section 400.1-301) indicates that parties can choose the governing law if the transaction has a reasonable relation to both states. However, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest that Missouri law might offer specific protections that Delaware law does not, particularly concerning contract interpretation or confidential information. If the contract has significant performance or involves consumers in Missouri, switching to Delaware law could render the choice of law unenforceable under Missouri law if it violates a fundamental policy of Missouri, creating a legal contradiction because the enforceability of clauses could be affected.

perturbation.original_text: Right to Enjoin Disclosure. The parties acknowledge that a Recipient's unauthorized disclosure or use of Proprietary Information may result in irreparable harm. Therefore, the parties agree that, in the event of violation or threatened violation of this Agreement, without limiting any other rights and remedies of each other, a temporary restraining order and/or an injunction to enjoin disclosure of Proprietary Information may be sought against the party who has breached or threatened to breach this Agreement and the party who has breached or threatened to breach this Agreement will not raise the defense of an adequate remedy at law.
perturbation.changed_text: Right to Enjoin Disclosure. The parties acknowledge that a Recipient's unauthorized disclosure or use of Proprietary Information may result in irreparable harm. Therefore, the parties agree that, in the event of violation or threatened violation of this Agreement, without limiting any other rights and remedies of each other, a temporary restraining order and/or an injunction to enjoin disclosure of Proprietary Information may be sought against the party who has breached or threatened to breach this Agreement; however, the party who has breached or threatened to breach this Agreement reserves all rights and defenses at law.
perturbation.explanation: The original text says the breaching party won't raise the defense of adequate remedy at law, the modification introduces contradiction by stating that the breaching party reserves all rights and defenses at law. The second part weakens the enforceability of the injunction clause, as it allows the breaching party to argue that monetary damages would be sufficient, thereby undermining the claim of irreparable harm.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 65 outlines the requirements for preliminary injunctions. By allowing the breaching party to reserve all rights and defenses, it creates uncertainty about whether the 'irreparable harm' requirement for an injunction is truly met, potentially contradicting the intent and practical application of Rule 65.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly waives the breaching party's right to raise the defense of an adequate remedy at law, reinforcing the availability of injunctive relief. The changed text directly contradicts this by reserving 'all rights and defenses at law,' thus allowing the breaching party to argue that monetary damages are sufficient, which undermines the irreparable harm claim needed for an injunction. The Law Explanation states that this 'creates uncertainty about whether the 'irreparable harm' requirement for an injunction is truly met.' Rule 65 addresses injunctions and restraining orders, indicating the legal basis for such remedies. While the snippets don't detail the 'irreparable harm' requirement specifically, they confirm that injunctions are governed by legal rules, and the change introduces uncertainty that would likely fail legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: All Proprietary Information must be retained by the Recipient in accordance with its customary procedures for handling confidential information of this nature and disclosed only to the Recipient's Affiliates or employees (or , attorneys, accountants and agents who have a non-disclosure obligation at least as restrictive as this Agreement) who need to know such information for purposes of the servicing or protection of its interest in respect of the Loans, the Credit Agreement and the Loan Documents (each as defined under the Credit Agreement) and the transactions contemplated thereby and to such third parties as the Discloser has consented to by prior written approval.
perturbation.changed_text: All Proprietary Information must be retained by the Recipient with no specific procedure for handling confidential information and disclosed to anyone the Recipient deems necessary. Disclosure to third parties requires no prior written approval.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates that confidential information be retained by the Recipient in accordance with its customary procedures for handling confidential information of this nature and disclosed only to the Recipient's Affiliates or employees with a non-disclosure agreement. The modification removes those restrictions, which weakens the confidentiality protection and increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) defines 'misappropriation' of a trade secret as disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent by a person who used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret. By removing the confidentiality protocols, the modification would enable the recipient to disclose the secret to any person for any reason, which violates the UTSA's intention to protect trade secrets through proper security measures and restricted access.
perturbation.justification: The original text strictly controls the disclosure of proprietary information, limiting it to affiliates, employees with NDAs, and approved third parties. The changed text removes these crucial confidentiality protocols. The Law Explanation highlights that the UTSA aims to protect trade secrets by restricting access and mandating proper security measures. While the scraped snippets are absent, the Perturbation Explanation, Law Explanation, and comparison of the original and changed texts reveal that the altered text directly contradicts the principles of trade secret protection under the UTSA. The removal of confidentiality safeguards, as prescribed in the original contract, results in a condition where unauthorized disclosure is more likely, weakening the intended level of security.
